title: Baily--Borel Compactifications of Period Images and the b-semiampleness Conjecture
speaker: Benjamin Thomas Bakker
abstract: Building on previous work of Satake and Baily, Baily and Borel proved in 1966 that arithmetic locally symmetric varieties admit canonical projective compactifications whose graded rings of functions are given by automorphic forms. Such varieties include moduli spaces of abelian varieties and have rich algebraic and arithmetic geometry. Griffiths suggested in 1970 that the same might be true for the image of any period map, which would then provide canonical compactifications of many more moduli spaces, including for instance those of Calabi--Yau varieties. In joint work with S. Filipazzi, M. Mauri, and J. Tsimerman, we confirm Griffiths' suggestion, and prove that the image of any period map admits a canonical functorial projective compactification. We also show how the same techniques yield a resolution to an important conjecture in birational geometry, the b-semiampleness conjecture of Prokhorov--Shokurov. Both proofs crucially use o-minimal GAGA, and the latter application additionally uses results of Ambro and Kollar on the geometry of minimal lc centers.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Classical Baily-Borel model for abelian varieties
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the Satake-Baily-Borel compactification of the moduli space of principally polarized abelian varieties, its construction from automorphic forms, its ample Hodge line bundle, its one-parameter boundary interpretation, and Borel's extension property.

proof_stages.label: Period images and their Baily-Borel compactification
proof_stages.summary: For a variation of Hodge structure, the period map has an algebraic image with ample Griffiths bundle, and the main compactification theorem constructs a projective Baily-Borel compactification as Proj of moderate-growth sections with the expected extension, polarization, and boundary-stratification properties.

proof_stages.label: Semiampleness criterion for Calabi-Yau variations
proof_stages.summary: Theorem 2 reduces semiampleness of determinant Hodge bundles and of the Griffiths bundle to the Calabi-Yau VHS case and characterizes it by integrability of the Hodge line plus torsion combinatorial monodromy.

proof_stages.label: Geometric verification and b-semiampleness
proof_stages.summary: For middle cohomology of families of KLT log Calabi-Yau varieties the two criterion hypotheses hold, so the Hodge bundle is semiample and this yields Kollár's b-semiampleness conjecture via the canonical bundle formula.

proof_stages.label: Quotient by Hodge-degree-zero curves
proof_stages.summary: To prove Theorem 2, the desired semiample morphism is first constructed topologically by quotienting X by chains of curves on which the Hodge bundle has degree zero, proving this is a proper algebraic equivalence relation compatible with the log stratification and algebraic on each stratum.

proof_stages.label: Local Hodge-theoretic separation of fibers
proof_stages.summary: A Hodge-theoretic step produces, locally along each quotient stratum, sections of powers of the Hodge bundle that separate fibers over points of that stratum, but these local sections alone do not control nearby deeper-boundary behavior.

proof_stages.label: Inductive algebraization by o-minimal GAGA
proof_stages.summary: Using global sections and ampleness on already-algebraized strata, one enlarges the algebraic locus stratum by stratum; definable analytic structures coming from Hodge theory are algebraized by o-minimal GAGA, and induction gives the semiample quotient.

examples.name: Hilbert modular surface pathologies
examples.note: Hilbert modular surfaces are used first to make possible a transcendental foliation along which only part of the Hodge structure is flat, and later to produce cusp rings with real-unit monodromy, illustrating that the two hypotheses in Theorem 2 are genuinely nonautomatic.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses Hilbert modular surfaces to exhibit failures of the two conditions, explaining the transcendental foliation/flatness issue and cusp rings with real-unit monodromy

passes.reason: Hilbert modular surfaces are used to exhibit failures of the integrability and torsion-combinatorial-monodromy conditions, but are not sporadic, collapsed, extremal, or maximally regular objects.
Hilbert modular surfaces are ordinary members of an infinite arithmetic family, here used to exhibit failures of integrability or torsion combinatorial monodromy.
Hilbert modular surfaces are locally symmetric and are used to exhibit failure of the desired integrability/torsion-monodromy conditions.

examples.name: Elliptic fibration canonical bundle formula
examples.note: A familiar elliptic surface fibration is used to explain the higher-dimensional canonical bundle formula by displaying the canonical, boundary, and moduli parts, with the moduli part identified with a Hodge bundle.
examples.engagement_reason: he invokes elliptic fibrations as the familiar case of the canonical bundle formula and states the splitting and meanings of its terms without computing a particular fibration

passes.reason: The elliptic fibration canonical bundle formula is presented as a standard motivating instance of the general canonical bundle formula, not as a failure case or structurally special object.

examples.name: Four local separation scenarios near strata
examples.note: The schematic pictures of neighborhoods of the stratified quotient show why sections produced only locally along a stratum do not suffice near deeper boundary strata, motivating the inductive algebraization step.
examples.engagement_reason: he draws and reasons through the local separation cases near strata, identifying which scenarios are handled and which require the inductive algebraization step

passes.reason: The local separation scenarios are used to display obstructive cases where local Hodge-theoretic sections do not yet give the desired analytic structure.

The local stratified separation pictures include boundary cases used as obstructions to the naive local-section construction.

The boundary-stratum separation configurations are edge cases in the induction and include obstructions where local sections do not separate fibers.
The local stratified pictures are ordinary proof cases, with the off-stratum and deeper-stratum cases serving as obstructions to a naive separation argument.
The stratified local boundary scenarios include lower-dimensional collapsed strata and are used as enemy cases where naive local separation of fibers is insufficient.

The boundary-stratum separation scenarios are enemy cases showing why local sections do not immediately give the desired analytic structure.
The stratified local pictures include adversarial cases showing why naive local separation of fibers is not enough near boundary strata.

The local strata-separation pictures include the obstructing cases showing why merely local Hodge-theoretic sections are insufficient.

examples.name: Nodal degeneration and minimal lc centers
examples.note: In response to a question, a two-branch degeneration picture with minimal log-canonical centers illustrates how Kollár's P1-linking identifies top forms birationally and yields torsion combinatorial monodromy in the Calabi-Yau case.
examples.engagement_reason: he sketches a nodal degeneration picture with minimal lc centers, tracks the limiting pieces and top-form identifications, and invokes P1-linking to get a birational/torsion conclusion

passes.reason: A nodal degeneration is a singular limiting case, and the minimal lc centers are minimal strata used positively to verify combinatorial monodromy.
